message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Targeted immunization or attacks of large-scale networks has attracted significant attention by the scientific community. However, in real-world scenarios, knowledge and observations of the network may be limited thereby precluding a full assessment of the optimal nodes to immunize (or remove) in order to avoid epidemic spreading such as that of current COVID-19 epidemic. Here, we study a novel immunization strategy where only
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  nodes are observed at a time and the most central between these
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  nodes is immunized (or attacked). This process is continued repeatedly until 1 −
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  fraction of nodes are immunized (or attacked). We develop an analytical framework for this approach and determine the critical percolation threshold
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  and the size of the giant component
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>∞;</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  for networks with arbitrary degree distributions
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                  ). In the limit of
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  → ∞ we recover prior work on targeted attack, whereas for
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  = 1 we recover the known case of random failure. Between these two extremes, we observe that as
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  increases,
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  increases quickly towards its optimal value under targeted immunization (attack) with complete information. In particular, we find a new scaling relationship between |
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (∞) −
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  ) | and
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  as |
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (∞) −
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  )| ~
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sup>−1</jats:sup>
                  exp(−
                  <jats:italic>αn</jats:italic>
                  ). For Scale-free (SF) networks, where
                  <jats:italic>P</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                  ) ~
                  <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sup>
                    −
                    <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sup>
                  , 2 <
                  <jats:italic>γ</jats:italic>
                  < 3, we find that
                  <jats:italic>p</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sub>
                  has a transition from zero to non-zero when
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  increases from
                  <jats:italic>n</jats:italic>
                  = 1 to order of log
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  is the size of network). Thus, for SF networks, knowledge of order of log
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  nodes and immunizing them can reduce dramatically an epidemics.
                </jats:p>
title: Efficient network immunization under limited knowledge

reference.unstructured: Reuven Cohen and Shlomo Havlin . Complex networks: structure, robustness and function. Cambridge University Press, 2010.

reference.article-title: Robustness of interdependent networks under targeted attack
Assessing the vulnerability of the fiber infrastructure to disasters

reference.unstructured: Dietrich Stauffer and Ammon Aharony. Introduction to percolation theory: revised second edition. CRC press, 2014.
H Eugene Stanley . Phase transitions and critical phenomena. Clarendon Press, Oxford, 1971.

reference.article-title: Robustness of network of networks under targeted attack
Recent advances on failure and recovery in networks of networks

reference.unstructured: Armin Bunde and Shlomo Havlin . Fractals and disordered systems. Springer Science & Business Media, 2012.

reference.article-title: Percolation points and critical point in the ising model
reference.journal-title: Journal of Physics A: Mathematical and General

reference.article-title: Spectrum of controlling and observing complex networks

reference.unstructured: Paul G Hoel et al. Introduction to mathematical statistics. Inc. New York, (2nd Ed), 1954.
